title: Introduction to Web Security
subtitle: Learn the habits that prevent the most common account and data breaches.

sections.heading: What Phishing Is
paragraphs: Phishing is a social attack where someone pretends to be trusted so you reveal passwords, codes, or payment details. The attacker relies on urgency and familiarity rather than technical exploitation.

Modern phishing often imitates login pages with convincing branding and domain names that differ by a single character. Always verify the sender, the URL, and whether the request makes sense for your role.
sections.difficultyHint: If a message demands immediate action, pause before clicking.
sections.simplifiedExplanation: Phishing is a fake message that tries to rush you into sharing sensitive information.
sections.shortExample: An email says your cloud account is locked and asks you to sign in through a link like g00gle-security.com.
sections.whyItMatters: A single phishing click can expose personal data or open access to an organization.

sections.heading: Strong Passwords
paragraphs: Password strength comes from length and uniqueness. A long passphrase that is different for every account is harder to crack and reduces damage when one service is breached.
Password managers make this practical by generating and storing unique credentials. Reusing one password across services creates a chain reaction when leaks happen.
sections.difficultyHint: A 4-word passphrase is usually better than a short complex word.
sections.simplifiedExplanation: Use long, unique passwords for each account and let a password manager remember them.
sections.shortExample: Instead of Re@d123, use a unique phrase like maple-river-cinema-signal with a manager.
sections.whyItMatters: Credential stuffing attacks depend on reused passwords.

sections.heading: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
paragraphs: MFA adds a second proof of identity beyond a password, such as an authenticator code, hardware key, or biometric check. This blocks many account takeovers when passwords are stolen.
Not all factors are equal. App-based authenticators and hardware keys are generally safer than SMS because phone numbers can be hijacked through SIM-swap attacks.
sections.difficultyHint: Think of MFA as an extra lock that still protects you if your password leaks.
sections.simplifiedExplanation: MFA asks for a second confirmation so stolen passwords alone are not enough.
sections.shortExample: You sign in with a password, then confirm with a code from your authenticator app.
sections.whyItMatters: MFA significantly reduces successful login abuse.

sections.heading: Social Engineering Tactics
paragraphs: Social engineering manipulates normal human behavior: trust, helpfulness, and fear. Attackers may call pretending to be IT support, ask for temporary access, or pressure you to bypass policy.
Defensive behavior is procedural. Verify identity through official channels, avoid sharing one-time codes, and normalize saying 'I will confirm this first.'
sections.difficultyHint: If someone asks for security exceptions, slow down and verify.
sections.simplifiedExplanation: Social engineering is psychological trickery, not a software bug.
sections.shortExample: A caller claims to be from IT and asks for your MFA code to 'fix sync issues.'
sections.whyItMatters: Even strong technical controls fail when users are manipulated into bypassing them.

sections.heading: Public Wi-Fi Risks
paragraphs: Open public networks can expose traffic to local attackers, especially on misconfigured or unencrypted services. Malicious hotspots can mimic legitimate names to capture data.
Use HTTPS websites, disable automatic network joining, and prefer a trusted VPN when handling sensitive tasks. Treat public Wi-Fi as an untrusted environment.
sections.difficultyHint: Assume anyone nearby could monitor unsafe traffic on open networks.
sections.simplifiedExplanation: Public Wi-Fi is convenient but should be treated like a shared public space.
sections.shortExample: Avoid logging into banking apps on an airport network unless protected with trusted encryption.
sections.whyItMatters: Network interception can expose credentials and session data.

questions.prompt: Which signal most strongly suggests a phishing attempt?

choices.text: The message asks for urgent login verification via a suspicious link.
The sender uses your first name.
The email contains a company logo.
The message was sent during business hours.

questions.explanation: Urgent pressure plus suspicious URLs is a core phishing pattern.

questions.prompt: Best first response to a suspicious account warning email is to:

choices.text: Click the email link and change your password immediately.
Reply to ask if the message is legitimate.
Open the service directly from a trusted bookmark and verify alerts there.
Forward it to colleagues for confirmation.

questions.explanation: Using trusted navigation avoids malicious links and confirms whether an alert is real.

questions.prompt: What improves account security the most?

choices.text: One complex password reused everywhere.
A unique long passphrase per account.
Changing the same password monthly.
Using personal details to make passwords memorable.

questions.explanation: Uniqueness prevents one breach from exposing multiple accounts.

choices.text: They let you keep one master password and generate unique credentials.
They eliminate the need for MFA.
They guarantee protection from phishing.
They only store passwords in browser memory.

questions.explanation: Managers reduce reuse by making unique passwords practical at scale.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of MFA?

choices.text: To speed up login by reducing password length.
To add another identity check beyond the password.
To replace account recovery.
To encrypt your web browser history.

questions.explanation: MFA mitigates stolen-password attacks by requiring another factor.

questions.prompt: Why can SMS MFA be weaker than app-based MFA?

choices.text: SMS codes expire too quickly.
Phone numbers can be hijacked via SIM swap.
SMS cannot be used internationally.
App-based MFA does not require internet.

questions.explanation: SIM swap attacks can redirect SMS codes to an attacker-controlled device.

questions.prompt: A caller asks for your one-time code to fix an urgent issue. Best action?

choices.text: Share the code if the caller sounds legitimate.
Decline and verify identity through official support channels.
Ask for their manager and then share.
Share only half the code.

questions.explanation: Verification through known channels prevents pretexting attacks.

choices.text: Cryptographic weaknesses
Human behavior and trust
Physical hardware damage
Software licensing errors

questions.explanation: The attack vector is psychological manipulation, not technical failure alone.

questions.prompt: Which habit is safest on public Wi-Fi?

choices.text: Disable HTTPS to increase speed.
Join any open network with a familiar name.
Use trusted encrypted connections and avoid sensitive tasks when possible.
Turn off firewall protections.

questions.explanation: Encryption and caution reduce interception risk on untrusted networks.

questions.prompt: Why is an evil twin hotspot dangerous?

choices.text: It provides stronger internet speed.
It mimics a trusted network name to capture traffic.
It blocks all websites except banking portals.
It can only affect mobile phones.

questions.explanation: Attackers can lure users onto fake networks to intercept data.
